title: Scramble string
description: Check if string is scrambled version

examples.explanation: 1. s1='great', s2='rgeat', not equal, lengths=5, sorted chars match. 2. Try k=1: No-swap: isScramble('g','r')=false, so skip. Swap: isScramble('g','t')=false, skip. 3. k=2: No-swap: isScramble('gr','rg') and isScramble('eat','eat'). Check 'gr' vs 'rg': k=1: No-swap: 'g' vs 'r'=false, Swap: 'g' vs 'g'=true and 'r' vs 'r'=true, so true. 'eat' vs 'eat'=true. So both true → return true.

1. s1='abcde', s2='caebd', not equal, lengths=5, sorted chars match. 2. k=1: No-swap: isScramble('a','c')=false. Swap: isScramble('a','d')=false. 3. k=2: No-swap: isScramble('ab','ca') and isScramble('cde','ebd'). 'ab' vs 'ca': k=1: 'a' vs 'c'=false, 'a' vs 'a'=true but 'b' vs 'c'=false, so false. Swap case also false. 4. k=3: No-swap: isScramble('abc','cae') and isScramble('de','bd'). 'abc' vs 'cae': k=1: 'a' vs 'c'=false, etc., all false. 5. k=4: Similar checks all false. Return false.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Recursion O(4^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively check all possible splits and swaps of the two strings to see if they can be scrambled. This approach explores every partition point and both swap and no-swap cases, making it exhaustive but inefficient.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n^2) due to recursion stack and substring operations

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Scramble String
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution checking all possible splits and swaps to determine if two strings are scrambled versions.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the scramble string problem by exploring every possible way the strings could have been scrambled. The intuition is that if s1 and s2 are scrambled versions, there must exist some split point k where either: 1) The first k characters of s1 match the first k characters of s2 (no swap case), and the remaining characters also match, OR 2) The first k characters of s1 match the last k characters of s2 (swap case), and the remaining characters match accordingly. This creates a recursive tree where at each level we try all possible split positions and both swap/no-swap possibilities. The algorithm: 1) Base cases: If strings are equal, return true. If lengths differ or character counts differ, return false. 2) For each split position k from 1 to n-1: a) No-swap case: Check if scramble(s1[0:k], s2[0:k]) AND scramble(s1[k:], s2[k:]) b) Swap case: Check if scramble(s1[0:k], s2[n-k:]) AND scramble(s1[k:], s2[0:n-k]) 3) Return true if any split works, else false. Time complexity is O(4^n) because each call generates 4 recursive calls (two cases × two subproblems). Space complexity is O(n^2) due to recursion depth (n) and substring operations (O(n) each). This connects to L1 as the complete implementation of the recursive logic. Common mistakes include: forgetting character count check (pruning), not handling empty strings properly, and missing the swap case. Real code pattern: def isScramble(s1, s2): if s1 == s2: return True; if sorted(s1) != sorted(s2): return False; n = len(s1); for k in range(1, n): if (isScramble(s1[:k], s2[:k]) and isScramble(s1[k:], s2[k:])) or (isScramble(s1[:k], s2[n-k:]) and isScramble(s1[k:], s2[:n-k])): return True; return False.

examples.trace: 1. s1='great', s2='rgeat', not equal, lengths=5, sorted chars match. 2. Try k=1: No-swap: isScramble('g','r')=false, so skip. Swap: isScramble('g','t')=false, skip. 3. k=2: No-swap: isScramble('gr','rg') and isScramble('eat','eat'). Check 'gr' vs 'rg': k=1: No-swap: 'g' vs 'r'=false, Swap: 'g' vs 'g'=true and 'r' vs 'r'=true, so true. 'eat' vs 'eat'=true. So both true → return true.

1. s1='abcde', s2='caebd', not equal, lengths=5, sorted chars match. 2. k=1: No-swap: isScramble('a','c')=false. Swap: isScramble('a','d')=false. 3. k=2: No-swap: isScramble('ab','ca') and isScramble('cde','ebd'). 'ab' vs 'ca': k=1: 'a' vs 'c'=false, 'a' vs 'a'=true but 'b' vs 'c'=false, so false. Swap case also false. 4. k=3: No-swap: isScramble('abc','cae') and isScramble('de','bd'). 'abc' vs 'cae': k=1: 'a' vs 'c'=false, etc., all false. 5. k=4: Similar checks all false. Return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Split and Compare Function
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that checks scramble condition for a given split.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach. The intuition is that to check if two strings are scrambled, we need to test all possible split points and both swap configurations recursively. The algorithm: 1) Take two strings s1 and s2. 2) If they are equal, return true (base case). 3) If their sorted versions differ, return false (pruning). 4) For each possible split position k (1 to n-1), recursively check: a) No-swap: Recursively check if s1[0:k] scrambles to s2[0:k] AND s1[k:] scrambles to s2[k:]. b) Swap: Recursively check if s1[0:k] scrambles to s2[n-k:] AND s1[k:] scrambles to s2[0:n-k]. 5) If any split returns true, return true; else false. Time complexity for this function is O(4^n) as it makes up to 4 recursive calls per split. Space complexity is O(n^2) due to recursion stack and substring copies. This connects to L0 as the main recursive function called in the full solution. Common mistakes: Incorrect index calculations for swap case, not pruning with character counts, and infinite recursion without proper base cases. Real code pattern: def checkScramble(s1, s2): if s1 == s2: return True; if sorted(s1) != sorted(s2): return False; n = len(s1); for k in range(1, n): if (checkScramble(s1[:k], s2[:k]) and checkScramble(s1[k:], s2[k:])) or (checkScramble(s1[:k], s2[n-k:]) and checkScramble(s1[k:], s2[:n-k])): return True; return False.

examples.trace: 1. s1='gr', s2='rg', not equal, sorted match. n=2. 2. k=1: No-swap: checkScramble('g','r') → 'g'!='r', sorted match? 'g' vs 'r' no, return false. Swap: checkScramble('g','g') → equal, return true AND checkScramble('r','r') → equal, return true. So true → return true.
1. s1='ab', s2='ac', not equal. 2. Check sorted: 'ab' sorted=['a','b'], 'ac' sorted=['a','c'], differ → return false immediately.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Split and Swap Recursive Pattern
ladders.desc: Understand the recursive pattern of splitting strings and checking swap/no-swap cases.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the recursive decomposition: to check if s1 scrambles to s2, split both strings at some point k, and recursively check two possibilities: no-swap (s1-left vs s2-left AND s1-right vs s2-right) or swap (s1-left vs s2-right AND s1-right vs s2-left). The intuition is that scrambling involves binary tree operations where at each node, we can either leave children in order or swap them. This creates a divide-and-conquer approach. Algorithm: 1) For strings s1 and s2 of length n, if n==1, just compare equality. 2) Otherwise, for each k from 1 to n-1, define: left1 = s1[0:k], right1 = s1[k:], left2 = s2[0:k], right2 = s2[k:], swap_left2 = s2[n-k:], swap_right2 = s2[0:n-k]. 3) Recursively solve: (solve(left1, left2) AND solve(right1, right2)) OR (solve(left1, swap_left2) AND solve(right1, swap_right2)). 4) Return true if any k yields true. Time complexity arises from trying all k and making 4 recursive calls, leading to O(4^n). Space is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L1 as the pattern implemented in the recursive function. Common mistakes: Confusing swap case indices, not considering all k values, and missing base cases. Real code pattern: def coreLogic(s1, s2): n = len(s1); if n == 1: return s1 == s2; for k in range(1, n): left1, right1 = s1[:k], s1[k:]; left2, right2 = s2[:k], s2[k:]; swap_left2, swap_right2 = s2[n-k:], s2[:n-k]; if (coreLogic(left1, left2) and coreLogic(right1, right2)) or (coreLogic(left1, swap_left2) and coreLogic(right1, swap_right2)): return True; return False.

examples.trace: 1. n=3. k=1: left1='e', right1='at'; left2='a', right2='te'; swap_left2='e', swap_right2='at'. No-swap: coreLogic('e','a')=false. Swap: coreLogic('e','e')=true AND coreLogic('at','at')=true → return true.

1. n=3. k=1: left1='1', right1='23'; left2='1', right2='32'; swap_left2='2', swap_right2='13'. No-swap: coreLogic('1','1')=true AND coreLogic('23','32') → check: n=2, k=1: '2' vs '3' false, '2' vs '2' true but '3' vs '3' false, so false. Swap: coreLogic('1','2')=false. k=2: left1='12', right1='3'; left2='13', right2='2'; swap_left2='32', swap_right2='1'. No-swap: coreLogic('12','13')=false. Swap: coreLogic('12','32') and coreLogic('3','1') → false. Return false? Wait, actually '123' and '132' are scrambled? Let's check: Split '123' as 1|23, swap to get 23|1 → '231', not '132'. Split as 12|3, no swap gives '123', swap gives '312'. Not matching. So output should be false. Correction: '123' vs '132' is false. Trace: k=1: No-swap: '1'='1' true, but '23' vs '32' false. Swap: '1' vs '2' false. k=2: No-swap: '12' vs '13' false. Swap: '12' vs '32' false. Return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — String Splitting and Substring Comparison
ladders.desc: Learn to split strings at all positions and compare substrings recursively.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the technique of splitting a string into two non-empty parts at every possible position and then operating on the substrings. The intuition is that many string problems, including scramble, require examining all partitions to find a valid configuration. Algorithm: 1) Given a string s of length n, generate all possible splits by choosing k from 1 to n-1. 2) For each k, create left substring s[0:k] and right substring s[k:]. 3) Perform some operation on left and right (e.g., compare to another string's substrings). 4) In scramble context, we compare left1 with left2 and right1 with right2, or left1 with swapped parts. Time complexity for generating splits is O(n) per string, but when combined with recursion, it leads to exponential complexity. Space complexity is O(n) for storing substrings. This connects to L2 as the mechanism for creating the split cases in the core logic. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in split ranges, not handling empty substrings (k must be 1 to n-1 to ensure non-empty), and inefficient substring copying. Real code pattern: def splitAndCompare(s1, s2): n = len(s1); for k in range(1, n): left1, right1 = s1[:k], s1[k:]; left2, right2 = s2[:k], s2[k:]; # Compare left1 with left2 and right1 with right2, or other combinations.

examples.trace: For s1='hello', n=5. k=1: left1='h', right1='ello'. k=2: left1='he', right1='llo'. k=3: left1='hel', right1='lo'. k=4: left1='hell', right1='o'. For s2='world', similar splits. Then compare: e.g., at k=2, left1='he' vs left2='wo' (false), etc.

n=2. k=1 only. left1='a', right1='b'; left2='b', right2='a'. Compare no-swap: 'a' vs 'b' false, 'b' vs 'a' false. Compare swap: left1 vs right2: 'a' vs 'a' true, right1 vs left2: 'b' vs 'b' true. So true.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Recursive Function with Base Cases
ladders.desc: Implement a simple recursive function that solves a smaller instance of a problem.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves writing a recursive function that calls itself with smaller inputs, using base cases to terminate. The intuition is recursion breaks a problem into subproblems of reduced size until trivial cases are reached. Algorithm: 1) Define function f(input). 2) Check base cases: if input is simple enough (e.g., empty string, single character), return a direct result. 3) Otherwise, reduce input size (e.g., split string, decrement number) and call f on the smaller inputs. 4) Combine results from recursive calls to form the answer. Time complexity depends on recursion tree; space complexity is O(depth) for call stack. This connects to L3 as recursion is used to handle split substrings. Common mistakes: Missing base cases causing infinite recursion, not reducing problem size properly, and stack overflow for deep recursion. Real code pattern: def recursiveFunc(s): if len(s) <= 1: return s; # Base case left = s[:len(s)//2]; right = s[len(s)//2:]; return combine(recursiveFunc(left), recursiveFunc(right)).

examples.trace: fact(5) calls fact(4) * 5. fact(4) calls fact(3) * 4. fact(3) calls fact(2) * 3. fact(2) calls fact(1) * 2. fact(1) returns 1 (base case). Then unwind: fact(2)=2*1=2, fact(3)=3*2=6, fact(4)=4*6=24, fact(5)=5*24=120.

reverse('abc'): if len<=1 return 'abc'? No, base: if empty return ''. Else: return reverse('bc') + 'a'. reverse('bc'): return reverse('c') + 'b'. reverse('c'): base return 'c'. Unwind: reverse('bc')='c'+'b'='cb', reverse('abc')='cb'+'a'='cba'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion and Base Cases
ladders.desc: Learn the fundamental idea of recursion: a function calling itself with simpler inputs.
ladders.explanation: Recursion is a programming technique where a function solves a problem by calling itself on smaller instances. The intuition is to break down a complex problem into similar but simpler subproblems until they become trivial to solve. Key components: 1) Base case: The simplest possible input where the answer is known directly, preventing infinite recursion. 2) Recursive case: The function calls itself with a reduced input, moving toward the base case. 3) Combination: Results from recursive calls are combined to solve the original problem. For example, to compute factorial(n): if n==0, return 1 (base case); else, return n * factorial(n-1) (recursive case). Time complexity often depends on the number of recursive calls; space complexity is the depth of the call stack. This connects to L4 as the foundation for writing recursive functions. Common mistakes: Forgetting base cases, not reducing input size (leading to infinite loops), and misunderstanding how recursive calls return values. Real code pattern: def recursiveExample(n): if n <= 0: return 0 # Base case; else: return n + recursiveExample(n-1).

examples.trace: Function countdown(n): if n<0 return; else print n; countdown(n-1). countdown(3): print 3; call countdown(2). countdown(2): print 2; call countdown(1). countdown(1): print 1; call countdown(0). countdown(0): print 0; call countdown(-1) which returns. Output: 3 2 1 0.
Function sum(n): if n==1 return 1 (base case); else return n + sum(n-1). sum(3): return 3 + sum(2). sum(2): return 2 + sum(1). sum(1): return 1. Unwind: sum(2)=2+1=3, sum(3)=3+3=6.

testCases.input: simple recursion: f(0)=1, f(n)=f(n-1)+1 for n=2
base case for string reversal of empty string
recursive check if number is even for 4

approaches.name: Approach 2: Memoized Recursion O(n^4)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to avoid redundant calculations by storing results of subproblems in a hash map. This reduces the exponential time complexity by caching outcomes for specific substrings and split points.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n^3) for memoization storage and recursion stack

ladders.title: Full Problem — Memoized Recursion for Scramble String
ladders.desc: Complete solution using memoized recursion to determine if one string is a scrambled version of another.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that a scrambled string can be formed by recursively swapping non-empty substrings. For strings s1 and s2, they are scrambled if there exists a split point k (1 ≤ k < n) such that either: (1) The first k characters of s1 are scrambled with the first k characters of s2, and the remaining n-k characters are scrambled with each other, OR (2) The first k characters of s1 are scrambled with the last k characters of s2, and the remaining n-k characters are scrambled with the first n-k characters of s2. This represents the two possible swap scenarios. To avoid exponential recomputation, we memoize results for subproblems defined by (i, j, length) where i is start index in s1, j is start index in s2, and length is the substring length. The base cases are: if lengths differ, return false; if strings are equal, return true; if sorted characters differ, return false (pruning). The algorithm recursively checks all possible splits, using memoization to store results. Time complexity is O(n^4) because for each subproblem we check O(n) splits and each check might involve O(n) character comparison, with O(n^3) subproblems. Space complexity is O(n^3) for memo storage plus O(n) recursion depth. This feeds from L1 by integrating the memoization helper into the full recursive logic. Common mistakes include not pruning with character frequency checks (leading to TLE), incorrect split ranges, or forgetting to handle both swap cases. Real code pattern: Define a recursive function solve(i, j, length) that checks memo, handles base cases, loops k from 1 to length-1, and returns memoized result.

examples.trace: 1. Call solve(0,0,5) for 'great' and 'rgeat'. 2. Check base: lengths 5=5, strings not equal, sorted chars same (aegrt). 3. Loop k=1 to 4. For k=2: Check no-swap: solve(0,0,2) for 'gr' and 'rg' AND solve(2,2,3) for 'eat' and 'eat'. solve(0,0,2): base: 'gr' != 'rg', sorted same (gr). Loop k=1: Check no-swap: solve(0,0,1) for 'g' and 'r' -> false (base: chars differ). Check swap: solve(0,1,1) for 'g' and 'g' -> true AND solve(1,0,1) for 'r' and 'r' -> true, so solve(0,0,2) returns true. solve(2,2,3): 'eat' == 'eat' -> true. Thus solve(0,0,5) returns true at k=2.

1. Call solve(0,0,5). 2. Base: lengths 5=5, not equal, sorted chars same (abcde). 3. Loop k=1 to 4. k=1: Check no-swap: solve(0,0,1) for 'a' and 'c' -> false (chars differ). Check swap: solve(0,4,1) for 'a' and 'd' -> false. k=2: Check no-swap: solve(0,0,2) for 'ab' and 'ca' -> false (after recursion). Check swap: solve(0,3,2) for 'ab' and 'bd' -> false. k=3: Check no-swap: solve(0,0,3) for 'abc' and 'cae' -> false. Check swap: solve(0,2,3) for 'abc' and 'ebd' -> false. k=4: Check no-swap: solve(0,0,4) for 'abcd' and 'caeb' -> false. Check swap: solve(0,1,4) for 'abcd' and 'aebd' -> false. All splits false, so return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Memoization Helper for Substring Scramble Check
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function with memoization that checks if substrings are scrambled.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is to create a helper function that, given start indices i in s1, j in s2, and a length, determines if the substring s1[i:i+length] is a scrambled version of s2[j:j+length]. This is the core recursive subproblem. We use a 3D memo dictionary keyed by (i, j, length) to store boolean results, avoiding recomputation. The function first checks memo; if found, returns the value. Then handles base cases: if length == 1, return s1[i] == s2[j]; if the substrings are equal, return true; if sorted characters of the substrings differ (checked via frequency array for efficiency), return false as pruning. Then, for each possible split point k from 1 to length-1, it checks two cases: (1) No swap: recursively check (i, j, k) AND (i+k, j+k, length-k). (2) Swap: recursively check (i, j+length-k, k) AND (i+k, j, length-k). If any split returns true, memoize and return true; else false. Time complexity for this subproblem is O(n^3) in total calls due to memoization, but each call does O(n) work for splits and checks, leading to O(n^4) overall. Space is O(n^3) for memo. This feeds into L0 as the main recursive engine. Common mistakes: incorrect memo key (e.g., using strings instead of indices), not pruning with character checks, or off-by-one errors in split indices. Real code pattern: Define function solve(i, j, length) with memo dict, base checks, loop for k, and return memo[(i,j,length)].

examples.trace: 1. Call solve(0,0,2). 2. Memo empty. 3. Base: length=2>1, substrings 'ab' != 'ba', sorted chars same (ab). 4. Loop k=1 only. Check no-swap: solve(0,0,1) for 'a' and 'b' -> false. Check swap: solve(0,1,1) for 'a' and 'a' -> true AND solve(1,0,1) for 'b' and 'b' -> true, so true. 5. Memoize (0,0,2)=true and return true.

1. Call solve(0,0,3). 2. Base: 'abc' != 'acb', sorted same (abc). 3. Loop k=1 to 2. k=1: no-swap: solve(0,0,1) for 'a' and 'a' -> true AND solve(1,1,2) for 'bc' and 'cb'. solve(1,1,2): base 'bc' != 'cb', sorted same. k=1: no-swap: solve(1,1,1) for 'b' and 'c' -> false; swap: solve(1,2,1) for 'b' and 'b' -> true AND solve(2,1,1) for 'c' and 'c' -> true, so solve(1,1,2)=true. Thus k=1 gives true, so solve(0,0,3)=true.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Split with Swap Cases for Scramble
ladders.desc: The central technique of checking all splits with two swap possibilities.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a scramble operation can be viewed as splitting a string into two non-empty parts and then optionally swapping them. For strings s1 and s2 of length n, they are scrambled if there exists a split at position k (1 ≤ k < n) such that either: (1) The first k chars of s1 are scrambled with first k chars of s2, and the last n-k chars are scrambled with each other (no swap), OR (2) The first k chars of s1 are scrambled with last k chars of s2, and the last n-k chars of s1 are scrambled with first n-k chars of s2 (swap). This captures all possible recursive scrambles. The algorithm iterates k from 1 to n-1, checking both cases recursively. This is the heart of the solution, reducing the problem to smaller subproblems. Time complexity for this logic alone, without memoization, is exponential O(2^n) due to two recursive calls per split. Space is O(n) for recursion depth. This feeds into L1 by being encapsulated in the helper function. Common mistakes: missing the swap case, incorrect index calculations for swapped parts, or not ensuring non-empty splits (k from 1 to n-1). Real code pattern: For k in 1 to length-1: if (solve(i,j,k) and solve(i+k,j+k,length-k)) or (solve(i,j+length-k,k) and solve(i+k,j,length-k)) return true.

examples.trace: 1. length=2, so k=1 only. 2. Check no-swap: solve(0,0,1) for 'g' and 'r' -> false. 3. Check swap: solve(0,1,1) for 'g' and 'g' -> true AND solve(1,0,1) for 'r' and 'r' -> true, so return true.

1. length=3, so k=1 and 2. k=1: no-swap: solve(0,0,1) for 'e' and 'a' -> false; swap: solve(0,2,1) for 'e' and 'e' -> true AND solve(1,0,2) for 'at' and 'at' -> true (since equal), so return true at k=1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Character Frequency Check for Pruning
ladders.desc: Intermediate concept of using character counts to quickly eliminate invalid subproblems.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that if two strings are scrambled, they must have the same multiset of characters. Therefore, before diving into recursive splits, we can check if the substrings have identical character frequencies; if not, they cannot be scrambled, and we can return false immediately. This pruning significantly reduces the search space. We implement this by creating frequency arrays of size 26 (for lowercase English letters), counting characters in both substrings, and comparing the arrays. Alternatively, we can sort the substrings and compare, but sorting is O(n log n) per check, while frequency array is O(n) with constant space. In the context of the scramble problem, this check is applied at each recursive call to avoid unnecessary recursion. Time complexity for this check is O(n) per subproblem, but it saves exponential work. Space is O(1) for the arrays. This feeds into L2 by being a precondition before split checks. Common mistakes: not resetting frequency arrays for each call, incorrect array size for other character sets, or forgetting to handle case sensitivity. Real code pattern: Create arrays freq1[26] and freq2[26], loop through length, increment based on s1[i+idx] and s2[j+idx], then compare arrays.

examples.trace: 1. Substrings: 'abc' and 'bca'. 2. freq1: a=1,b=1,c=1; freq2: a=1,b=1,c=1. 3. Arrays equal, so pruning passes and recursion proceeds.
1. Substrings: 'abc' and 'abd'. 2. freq1: a=1,b=1,c=1; freq2: a=1,b=1,d=1. 3. Arrays differ at indices for c and d, so pruning returns false immediately.

testCases.expected: false (length mismatch, but assume lengths equal for check)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Substring Extraction and Comparison
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of working with substrings via indices.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that in the scramble problem, we frequently need to refer to substrings of the original strings without creating new string objects (to save time and space). We use start indices and length to represent substrings implicitly. For example, substring of s1 starting at index i with length L is s1[i:i+L]. Basic operations include: checking if two substrings are equal by comparing characters from i to i+L-1 and j to j+L-1; accessing individual characters for frequency checks; and passing indices to recursive calls. This avoids O(n) substring copying at each step. The algorithm relies on these index manipulations to define subproblems. Time complexity for equality check is O(L), and for character access is O(1). Space is O(1) extra. This feeds into L3 by providing the data for frequency checks. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in index ranges (e.g., using i+L instead of i+L-1), not validating indices within bounds, or accidentally modifying strings. Real code pattern: Use loops like for idx in range(length): if s1[i+idx] != s2[j+idx]: return false for equality; or freq[ord(s1[i+idx]) - ord('a')] += 1 for frequency.

examples.input: s1='hello', i=1, length=3, s2='world', j=2, length=3
examples.trace: 1. Substring1: s1[1:4] = 'ell'. 2. Substring2: s2[2:5] = 'rld'. 3. Compare char by char: idx=0: 'e' vs 'r' -> differ, so return false.

examples.input: s1='abcde', i=2, length=2, s2='cdefg', j=0, length=2
examples.trace: 1. Substring1: s1[2:4] = 'cd'. 2. Substring2: s2[0:2] = 'cd'. 3. Compare: idx=0: 'c'=='c'; idx=1: 'd'=='d'; so return true.

testCases.input: s1='test', i=0, length=4, s2='test', j=0, length=4
s1='test', i=0, length=4, s2='tset', j=0, length=4

s1='abcdef', i=0, length=2, s2='abcdef', j=4, length=2

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Recursion with Base Cases
ladders.desc: Simplest prerequisite: understanding recursive function calls and base conditions.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that recursion is a technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of the same problem. It requires two key components: a base case that stops the recursion (to prevent infinite loops) and a recursive case that breaks the problem down. For example, to check if two strings are equal recursively, we can define a function that compares the first characters and then calls itself on the remaining substrings. In the context of scramble strings, recursion is used to handle substrings of decreasing length. Base cases include: when length is 1, compare the single characters; when strings are equal, return true; when lengths differ or character sets differ, return false. This foundation is essential because the memoized approach builds upon recursive decomposition. Time complexity without optimization is exponential, but it introduces the concept of problem reduction. Space complexity is O(n) for the call stack. This feeds into L4 by providing the structural framework for substring operations. Common mistakes: missing base cases leading to stack overflow, incorrect recursive parameters, or not handling edge cases like empty strings. Real code pattern: Define function solve(i, j, length): if length == 1: return s1[i] == s2[j]; else: handle recursive splits.

examples.trace: 1. Call solve(0,0,2). 2. Base: length=2>1, so not base. 3. Recursive case: split k=1. Check no-swap: solve(0,0,1) for 'h' and 'i' -> false. Check swap: solve(0,1,1) for 'h' and 'h' -> true AND solve(1,0,1) for 'i' and 'i' -> true, so return true.

1. Call solve(0,0,2). 2. Base: length=2>1. 3. Recursive: k=1. No-swap: solve(0,0,1) for 'n' and 'o' -> false. Swap: solve(0,1,1) for 'n' and 'n' -> true AND solve(1,0,1) for 'o' and 'o' -> true, so true.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming O(n^4)
approaches.summary: Build a 4D DP table where dp[i][j][k] indicates if substrings of length k starting at i in s1 and j in s2 are scrambled. Iterate over all lengths and split points to fill the table based on previous results.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Scramble String with Bottom-Up DP
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking if s2 is a scrambled version of s1 using 4D DP table.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the scramble string problem using bottom-up dynamic programming with O(n⁴) time complexity. The key insight is that two strings are scrambled if they can be split at some point where either: 1) The left parts are scrambled and right parts are scrambled (no swap), OR 2) The left part of s1 is scrambled with right part of s2 and right part of s1 is scrambled with left part of s2 (swap). We build a 4D DP table dp[i][j][k] where i is starting index in s1, j is starting index in s2, and k is length. The table stores whether substring s1[i:i+k] is a scrambled version of s2[j:j+k]. We fill the table for all lengths from 1 to n, for all starting positions i and j, and for all possible split points within each length. For base case: dp[i][j][1] = (s1[i] == s2[j]). For length k > 1, we check all split points q from 1 to k-1. For each split, we check two conditions: 1) No swap: dp[i][j][q] && dp[i+q][j+q][k-q] 2) With swap: dp[i][j+k-q][q] && dp[i+q][j][k-q]. If either condition is true for any split point, dp[i][j][k] = true. The final answer is dp[0][0][n]. Time complexity is O(n⁴) because we have three nested loops for i, j, k (each O(n)) and an inner loop for split points (O(n)). Space complexity is O(n³) for the 3D DP table (i, j, k dimensions). Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle the swap case, incorrect indexing for substring boundaries, and not optimizing memory usage for large n. This approach is more efficient than recursion with memoization for worst-case scenarios as it avoids recursion overhead.

examples.trace: n=5. Initialize dp[5][5][6] (0-indexed, k from 1 to 5). Base cases: dp[i][j][1] = (s1[i]==s2[j]). For k=2: Check all i,j. Example: i=0,j=0,k=2 (substrings 'gr' and 'rg'). Check splits q=1: No swap: dp[0][0][1]&&dp[1][1][1] = ('g'=='r')&&('r'=='g') = false. Swap: dp[0][1][1]&&dp[1][0][1] = ('g'=='g')&&('r'=='r') = true. So dp[0][0][2]=true. Similarly fill all k=2. For k=3: Example i=0,j=0,k=3 ('gre','rge'). Check q=1: No swap: dp[0][0][1]&&dp[1][1][2] = false&&? need compute dp[1][1][2] first. q=2: No swap: dp[0][0][2]&&dp[2][2][1] = true&&('e'=='e')=true. So dp[0][0][3]=true. Continue for all. Finally dp[0][0][5] computes to true.

n=5. Base cases: dp[i][j][1] for all i,j. For k=2: Check all. Example i=0,j=0,k=2 ('ab','ca'): q=1: No swap: dp[0][0][1]&&dp[1][1][1] = ('a'=='c')&&('b'=='a')=false. Swap: dp[0][1][1]&&dp[1][0][1] = ('a'=='a')&&('b'=='c')=false. So false. Similarly compute all. For k=3: Example i=0,j=0,k=3 ('abc','cae'): Check q=1,2. q=1: No swap: dp[0][0][1]&&dp[1][1][2] = false&&? dp[1][1][2] for 'bc' and 'ae' is false. q=2: No swap: dp[0][0][2]&&dp[2][2][1] = false&&('c'=='e')=false. Swap cases also false. So false. Continue. Eventually dp[0][0][5] evaluates to false after checking all splits.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Table Filling for Fixed Length
ladders.desc: Fill DP table for a specific substring length k using previously computed smaller lengths.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the bottom-up DP approach. Given that we have already computed DP values for all lengths smaller than k, we now compute dp[i][j][k] for all possible starting indices i and j. The intuition is that to determine if substrings of length k starting at i in s1 and j in s2 are scrambled, we need to check all possible ways to split these substrings. For each split point q (1 ≤ q < k), we consider two scenarios: 1) No swap: The first q characters of s1's substring are scrambled with the first q characters of s2's substring, AND the remaining k-q characters are also scrambled. This corresponds to dp[i][j][q] && dp[i+q][j+q][k-q]. 2) With swap: The first q characters of s1's substring are scrambled with the last q characters of s2's substring, AND the remaining k-q characters of s1 are scrambled with the first k-q characters of s2. This corresponds to dp[i][j+k-q][q] && dp[i+q][j][k-q]. If for any split point q, either of these conditions is true, then dp[i][j][k] = true. Otherwise, it's false. The algorithm: For each i from 0 to n-k, for each j from 0 to n-k, initialize dp[i][j][k] = false. Then for q from 1 to k-1, compute no_swap = dp[i][j][q] && dp[i+q][j+q][k-q] and with_swap = dp[i][j+k-q][q] && dp[i+q][j][k-q]. If no_swap or with_swap is true, set dp[i][j][k] = true and break. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n³) because we have loops for i, j, and q, each up to O(n). Space complexity is O(1) additional space, but we're using the existing DP table. This feeds into the full problem by being the main step repeated for each length k from 2 to n. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in indices (remember strings are 0-indexed), not breaking early when a true condition is found (optimization), and incorrectly handling edge cases where indices go out of bounds (ensure i+q, j+k-q, etc. are within bounds).

examples.trace: We're computing dp[0][0][2] for substrings 'ab' and 'ba'. k=2, so possible split q=1. Check no_swap: dp[0][0][1] && dp[1][1][1] = false && false = false. Check with_swap: dp[0][1][1] && dp[1][0][1] = true && true = true. Since with_swap is true, set dp[0][0][2] = true.
Compute dp[0][0][2] for 'ab' and 'ab'. q=1. No_swap: dp[0][0][1] && dp[1][1][1] = true && true = true. So set dp[0][0][2] = true. We don't need to check with_swap since we already found true.

testCases.input: s1='a', s2='a', i=0, j=0, k=1, base dp
s1='a', s2='b', i=0, j=0, k=1, base dp
s1='ab', s2='ba', i=0, j=0, k=2, with dp as in example
s1='abc', s2='bca', i=0, j=0, k=3, with appropriate dp values
s1='abc', s2='cab', i=0, j=0, k=3, with appropriate dp values
s1='abc', s2='acb', i=0, j=0, k=3, with appropriate dp values
s1='abcd', s2='badc', i=0, j=0, k=4, with appropriate dp values
s1='abcd', s2='abdc', i=0, j=0, k=4, with appropriate dp values
s1='great', s2='rgeat', i=0, j=0, k=5, with appropriate dp values

ladders.title: Core Logic — Scramble Condition with Split Points
ladders.desc: Determine if two substrings are scrambled by checking all possible split points.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight of the scramble string problem: two strings s1 and s2 are scrambled if there exists a split point where either the substrings are scrambled without swapping or with swapping. Formally, for strings of length k, they are scrambled if there exists a q (1 ≤ q < k) such that: (s1[0:q] is scrambled with s2[0:q] AND s1[q:k] is scrambled with s2[q:k]) OR (s1[0:q] is scrambled with s2[k-q:k] AND s1[q:k] is scrambled with s2[0:k-q]). This recursive definition allows us to break down the problem into smaller subproblems. The intuition comes from the operation allowed in scrambling: we can recursively split a string into two non-empty substrings and optionally swap them. Therefore, to check if two strings are scrambled, we need to consider all possible first splits. The algorithm: Given substrings s1_sub and s2_sub of length k, for each split point q from 1 to k-1, compute two checks: 1) Check no swap: recursively check if first q chars of s1_sub are scrambled with first q chars of s2_sub, and last k-q chars are scrambled. 2) Check with swap: recursively check if first q chars of s1_sub are scrambled with last q chars of s2_sub, and last k-q chars of s1_sub are scrambled with first k-q chars of s2_sub. If any q yields true in either check, return true. Otherwise, return false. Time complexity for this logic on strings of length k is O(k) if we assume recursive calls are O(1), but in practice they lead to exponential time without memoization. Space complexity is O(k) for recursion depth. This feeds into the DP approach by providing the recurrence relation that we use to fill the DP table. Common mistakes include: not considering all split points, forgetting the swap case, and not handling base cases (length 1) properly. This logic is why the problem has O(n⁴) complexity in DP: we check O(n) splits for each of O(n³) subproblems.

examples.trace: Length k=2. Possible split q=1. Check no swap: Compare 'a' with 'b' and 'b' with 'a' -> false. Check with swap: Compare 'a' with 'a' and 'b' with 'b' -> true. So return true.
k=2. q=1. No swap: 'a' with 'a' true, 'b' with 'c' false -> overall false. With swap: 'a' with 'c' false, 'b' with 'a' false -> false. No other splits, so return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Substring Comparison and Splitting
ladders.desc: Extract and compare substrings at given indices and lengths, and split them into parts.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves manipulating string substrings based on indices and lengths, which is fundamental to implementing the scramble condition. Given a string s, a starting index i, and a length k, the substring is s[i:i+k] (using Python slicing notation). When checking the scramble condition, we need to compare substrings from s1 and s2, and also consider split points. For a split point q (1 ≤ q < k), we split the substring into two parts: left part of length q (indices i to i+q-1) and right part of length k-q (indices i+q to i+k-1). Similarly for the other string. The key operations are: 1) Extracting substrings: s1[i:i+k] and s2[j:j+k]. 2) Splitting: For split q, left1 = s1[i:i+q], right1 = s1[i+q:i+k], left2 = s2[j:j+q], right2 = s2[j+q:j+k] for no-swap case; for swap case, left2_swap = s2[j+k-q:j+k], right2_swap = s2[j:j+k-q]. 3) Comparing: Check if left1 and left2 are scrambled, and right1 and right2 are scrambled. The intuition is that these operations allow us to break down the problem into smaller pieces. In terms of algorithm, we use loops or recursive calls over these substrings. Time complexity for extracting a substring is O(k) if done naively, but in practice we can pass indices to avoid copying strings. Space complexity is O(k) if we create new strings, but O(1) if we use indices. This feeds into the core logic by providing the mechanism to apply the scramble condition to specific parts of the strings. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in substring indices (remember that slicing is exclusive of the end index), not handling empty substrings (q must be between 1 and k-1 to ensure non-empty parts), and inefficiency from creating many substring objects. In the DP approach, we avoid substring copies by using indices into the original strings.

examples.trace: String s='great', start i=0, length k=5. Substring s[0:5]='great'. Split at q=2. Left part: indices i to i+q-1 = 0 to 1, so s[0:2]='gr'. Right part: indices i+q to i+k-1 = 2 to 4, so s[2:5]='eat'.

examples.input: s='rgeat', j=0, k=5, q=2 for swap case with total length 5
examples.trace: String s='rgeat', start j=0, length k=5. For swap case, we consider last q chars as left: indices j+k-q to j+k-1 = 5-2 to 4 = 3 to 4, so s[3:5]='at'. Right part: first k-q chars: indices j to j+k-q-1 = 0 to 2, so s[0:3]='rge'.

testCases.input: s='test', i=0, k=4, q=1 for swap with k=4
s='example', i=0, k=7, q=4 for swap with k=7

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Indexing and Slicing
ladders.desc: Access characters and extract substrings from a string using indices.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves working with strings as sequences of characters, where each character has an index starting from 0. String indexing allows accessing a single character at a specific position, e.g., s[i] returns the character at index i. String slicing extracts a contiguous subsequence from index a to b-1, written as s[a:b]. If a is omitted, it defaults to 0 (start of string); if b is omitted, it defaults to len(s) (end of string). Negative indices count from the end, e.g., s[-1] is the last character. The intuition is that strings are immutable sequences, so indexing and slicing create new strings or characters without modifying the original. This is crucial for the scramble problem because we need to compare parts of strings. The algorithm for slicing: given string s, start index a, and end index b, return a new string containing characters s[a], s[a+1], ..., s[b-1]. If a ≥ b, returns empty string. Time complexity is O(b-a) for slicing as it copies characters. Space complexity is O(b-a) for the new string. This feeds into substring operations by providing the basic tool to extract parts of strings for comparison. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors (remember slicing is exclusive at the end), using indices out of bounds (which raises an error in most languages), and inefficiency from excessive slicing in loops. In the DP approach, we minimize slicing by passing indices instead of creating substrings.

examples.trace: String 'hello' has indices: 0='h', 1='e', 2='l', 3='l', 4='o'. s[1] accesses index 1: 'e'. s[1:4] slices from index 1 inclusive to 4 exclusive: indices 1,2,3 -> 'e','l','l' so 'ell'.
String 'dynamic': indices 0='d',1='y',2='n',3='a',4='m',5='i',6='c'. s[2:5] takes indices 2,3,4 -> 'n','a','m' so 'nam'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Strings as Sequences
ladders.desc: Understand strings as ordered collections of characters with properties like length and indexing.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a string is a data structure that represents a sequence of characters. Characters are symbols like letters, digits, or punctuation. Strings have a length, which is the number of characters they contain, denoted as len(s). They are typically immutable, meaning once created, the characters cannot be changed directly. The order of characters matters: 'ab' is different from 'ba'. This is fundamental to the scramble problem because scrambling involves rearranging characters while potentially changing the order. The intuition is that we can think of a string as a list or array of characters, where each position holds a character. Basic operations include: creating a string (e.g., s = 'hello'), getting its length (len(s)), and iterating over characters (e.g., for char in s). In programming, strings are often 0-indexed, meaning the first character is at position 0, the second at position 1, and so on. This indexing allows us to refer to specific characters. Time complexity for accessing length is O(1) in most languages. Space complexity for a string is O(n) where n is the number of characters. This feeds into string indexing and slicing by providing the basic understanding of how strings are structured. Common mistakes include: confusing strings with lists (strings are immutable, lists are mutable), forgetting that spaces and punctuation are also characters, and not understanding 0-indexing. For beginners, it's important to practice with simple strings to build intuition.

examples.trace: String 'cat' has 3 characters. len('cat') returns 3. The characters are: index 0: 'c', index 1: 'a', index 2: 't'. We can access them as s[0], s[1], s[2].
String 'dog' has length 3. When iterating, e.g., in a loop: for char in s: print(char), it prints 'd', then 'o', then 'g'.
